results.explanation: The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, with write bandwidth close to the peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is saturated. The high I/O time ratio and low CPU utilization further confirm that I/O throughput limits performance rather than compute or network. The workload issues large sequential writes, which aligns with bandwidth saturation rather than metadata or lock contention.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, with write bandwidth close to the peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is saturated. The workload issues large sequential writes with low IOPS, and network utilization is minimal, ruling out network or metadata bottlenecks. High I/O time ratio confirms I/O is the dominant factor, consistent with bandwidth saturation.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, with read bandwidth close to the peak storage bandwidth, indicating the storage device is nearly saturated. The large average I/O size and high sequential ratio suggest efficient large reads, so the bottleneck is the maximum throughput of the storage system. Network and CPU utilization are low, ruling out other bottlenecks.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 96% of peak capacity, indicating the storage device is nearly saturated. The workload issues large sequential writes with a high average IO size and low IOPS, consistent with bandwidth-bound behavior. Network and CPU utilization are low, ruling out network or compute bottlenecks. Therefore, the primary bottleneck is storage bandwidth saturation.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 96%, close to the peak device bandwidth, indicating the storage device is saturated. The I/O time dominates the stage execution (85%), while CPU and network utilization are low, ruling out compute or network bottlenecks. The workload performs large sequential reads, consistent with bandwidth saturation rather than metadata or lock contention.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, indicating the storage device is nearly saturated. The workload issues large sequential writes with a high average IO size and low IOPS, consistent with bandwidth-bound behavior. CPU and network utilization are low, ruling out compute or network bottlenecks. Therefore, the primary bottleneck is storage bandwidth saturation.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very high at 95%, close to the peak capacity of 150 MB/s, indicating the storage device is saturated. The high I/O time ratio (82%) and low CPU utilization (25%) further confirm that the workflow is waiting on I/O throughput. Other potential bottlenecks like network or metadata contention are not supported by the metrics.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O with very low bandwidth utilization and many small I/O operations (4 KB average size) at moderate IOPS, indicating many small file operations. The storage is shared NFS with a single stripe, which often leads to metadata server contention when many small writes occur concurrently. Low CPU and network utilization further support that metadata overhead is the primary bottleneck.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O (84%) but bandwidth utilization is very low (1.5%), indicating bandwidth is not saturated. The high IOPS with very small average I/O size (8 KB) and low sequential ratio suggest many small metadata operations. Low CPU and network utilization further exclude compute or network bottlenecks. The shared NFS with a single stripe and many small file operations point to metadata server contention as the primary bottleneck.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O but achieves very low bandwidth utilization with many small (2 KB) reads and high IOPS, indicating many tiny file operations. Low CPU and network utilization exclude compute or network bottlenecks. The shared NFS storage with a single stripe and many small operations suggests metadata server contention is limiting performance.

The workflow shows very high I/O time ratio with low bandwidth utilization and many small I/O operations (32 KB average size) at moderate IOPS, indicating many small reads. The low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism suggests metadata server overhead is limiting performance rather than raw data transfer. This pattern is typical of metadata contention from numerous small file operations.

The workflow spends most of its time in I/O (85%) but the bandwidth utilization is very low (1.5%) with very small average I/O size (4 KB) and relatively high IOPS, indicating many small file operations. CPU and network utilization are low, and storage bandwidth is not saturated, pointing to metadata server contention due to numerous small reads on a shared NFS storage.

The workflow spends a large portion of time in I/O (81%) but achieves very low bandwidth utilization (4%) with many small I/O operations (16 KB) and relatively high IOPS (768). This pattern indicates that the metadata server is overwhelmed by numerous small writes, causing metadata contention rather than bandwidth saturation or network bottlenecks.

The workflow exhibits very high I/O time ratio but extremely low bandwidth utilization, combined with many small (1 KB) write operations and moderate IOPS. This pattern indicates that the metadata server is overwhelmed by numerous small file operations rather than bandwidth saturation. The shared NFS storage and many parallel writers exacerbate metadata contention.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many parallel tasks, the write bandwidth is low relative to peak storage bandwidth, indicating inefficient I/O. The presence of a single shared output file with 200 concurrent writers suggests heavy lock contention limiting effective write throughput.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many parallel tasks, the write bandwidth utilization is low relative to peak storage bandwidth, indicating inefficient I/O. The presence of 64 writers per shared file suggests heavy contention for write locks, which limits effective parallel write throughput and causes serialization delays. This lock contention on shared output files is the primary bottleneck.

Despite high parallelism with 300 tasks writing to a single shared file, the write bandwidth is low relative to peak storage bandwidth, indicating contention. The large number of writers per shared file causes lock contention, limiting effective I/O throughput. The high I/O time ratio confirms I/O is the bottleneck, but bandwidth is underutilized due to serialization from lock contention.

Despite high parallelism and many writers (64) targeting a single shared output file, the write bandwidth utilization is low (21%) while I/O time dominates execution (82%). This indicates multiple tasks are contending for write locks on the shared file, causing serialization and limiting effective bandwidth. The large average I/O size and low IOPS exclude metadata contention, and network and storage bandwidth are underutilized.

Despite many tasks writing in parallel with large IO sizes and low storage bandwidth utilization, the write bandwidth is low relative to peak capacity. The presence of 64 writers per shared file (only 4 shared files) strongly suggests multiple tasks are contending for write locks, causing serialization and reduced effective bandwidth. This lock contention limits throughput even though the storage and network are underutilized.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many parallel tasks, the write bandwidth is low relative to peak storage bandwidth, indicating inefficient I/O. The presence of only 2 shared output files with 200 writers each suggests heavy lock contention on shared files, limiting effective parallel write throughput. Low CPU utilization confirms the bottleneck is I/O related, specifically due to multiple writers competing for locks.

Despite high parallelism and many concurrent writers (167 per shared file) on only 3 shared output files, the write bandwidth utilization is low (22%) and CPU utilization is low, indicating I/O is the bottleneck. The low bandwidth despite active I/O and many writers suggests heavy lock contention on shared files limiting effective parallel writes.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.1) compared to the peak capacity, indicating storage is not saturated. However, the network utilization ratio is very high (0.95), showing the network is nearly fully utilized. Since the workflow reads from S3 (remote object storage), the network is the limiting factor in data transfer, causing the high I/O time ratio despite low storage bandwidth usage.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.1) compared to the network utilization (0.95), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The read bandwidth matches the network bandwidth, showing data transfer is constrained by network capacity rather than storage. Low CPU utilization further supports that the bottleneck is I/O network transfer, not compute.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) while the network utilization is very high (0.95), indicating that data transfer over the network is the limiting factor. The workflow reads from an S3 object store, which relies heavily on network throughput. High I/O time ratio and low CPU utilization further support that network I/O is the primary bottleneck.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) compared to the network utilization which is very high (0.94), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The workflow reads large sequential chunks from an S3 object store, and the network bandwidth is nearly saturated while storage bandwidth is underutilized. Low CPU utilization further supports that the bottleneck is in data transfer over the network rather than compute or storage.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) compared to the high network utilization (0.94), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The workflow uses S3 object storage, which relies heavily on network transfers. High I/O time ratio with low CPU utilization further supports that data movement over the network is the primary bottleneck.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.1), indicating the storage device is not saturated. However, the network utilization ratio is very high (0.95), and the network bandwidth matches the read bandwidth, showing that data transfer over the network is the limiting factor. This suggests the workflow is bottlenecked by network I/O rather than storage or compute.

The storage bandwidth utilization is very low (0.09) while the network utilization is very high (0.95), indicating the network is the limiting factor. The workload reads from remote S3 storage, and the network bandwidth closely matches the read bandwidth, confirming data transfer over the network is the bottleneck. CPU utilization is low, so compute is not limiting.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many tasks, the I/O parallelism is limited to a single writer/reader, severely restricting throughput and causing low bandwidth utilization. The low CPU utilization and network usage further indicate that the bottleneck is due to serialization of I/O rather than compute, network, or storage bandwidth saturation.

The I/O parallelism is limited to a single writer/reader, which prevents the workflow from fully utilizing available storage bandwidth. Despite low bandwidth utilization and low CPU usage, the high I/O time ratio indicates that I/O is the bottleneck, caused by serialization of I/O operations rather than bandwidth saturation or metadata contention.

The I/O is highly serialized with only one I/O worker and parallelism of 1, severely limiting throughput despite low storage bandwidth utilization. The high I/O time ratio and low CPU utilization indicate the workflow is waiting on I/O that cannot be parallelized. This serialization prevents the system from leveraging available storage bandwidth and network capacity.

Despite a high IO time ratio and low CPU utilization, the bandwidth utilization is very low and IO parallelism is limited to a single worker. This indicates that I/O is serialized through a single writer, preventing the workflow from leveraging available storage bandwidth and parallelism, causing the primary bottleneck.

Despite many tasks and high I/O time ratio, the bandwidth utilization is low and io_parallelism is 1, indicating all I/O is serialized through a single writer. This serialization prevents the workflow from leveraging available storage bandwidth and parallelism, causing the primary bottleneck.

Despite high I/O time ratio and many tasks, the bandwidth utilization is very low and io_parallelism is 1, indicating all I/O is serialized through a single writer. This serialization prevents parallel I/O and limits throughput, causing the I/O phase to dominate execution time.

The I/O is highly serialized with only one I/O worker and parallelism of 1, severely limiting throughput despite low storage bandwidth utilization. The high I/O time ratio and low CPU utilization indicate the workflow is waiting on this serialized I/O. Increasing parallel I/O would likely improve performance.

The workflow spends over half its time in I/O with low storage bandwidth utilization, indicating I/O is active but not saturated. The large, frequent checkpoints (8000 MB every 300s, 12 times) dominate I/O time, causing overhead. Low CPU utilization confirms the bottleneck is not compute-bound, and low bandwidth utilization rules out storage saturation or network bottlenecks.

The workflow spends a majority of time on I/O (58%) dominated by large, frequent checkpoint writes (15 checkpoints of 15GB each). Despite low storage bandwidth utilization (12%) and low CPU usage (8%), the large checkpoint size and frequency cause significant overhead. This indicates checkpointing overhead is the primary bottleneck rather than bandwidth saturation or compute limits.

The workflow spends half of its time on I/O, dominated by large, frequent checkpoint writes totaling 180 GB over 6 intervals. Despite moderate bandwidth utilization and low CPU usage, the periodic large checkpointing operations cause significant overhead, limiting overall progress.

The workflow spends a large fraction of time on I/O (44%) dominated by frequent, large checkpoint writes (8 GB every 2 minutes, 30 times). Storage bandwidth utilization is moderate, and CPU utilization is low, indicating I/O overhead from checkpointing is the primary bottleneck rather than bandwidth saturation or compute limits.

The workflow spends over half its time on I/O dominated by large, frequent checkpoints (8000 MB every 300s, 12 times total). Despite low storage bandwidth utilization and low CPU usage, the high checkpointing overhead causes significant I/O time, indicating that checkpointing dominates stage execution rather than bandwidth saturation or compute limits.

The workflow spends a significant portion of time on I/O (40%) with large, frequent checkpoints (100 GB every 900 seconds, 8 total). The aggregate checkpoint data (800 GB) dominates the I/O volume, causing substantial overhead. Storage bandwidth is underutilized, and network is not a bottleneck, indicating the main limitation is the cost of checkpointing itself.

The workflow spends 75% of its time on I/O dominated by frequent large checkpoint writes (8 GB every 180 seconds, 20 times total). Despite low storage bandwidth utilization (15%) and low CPU usage, the high checkpointing overhead causes significant I/O time. This indicates that periodic checkpointing is the primary bottleneck rather than bandwidth saturation or compute limits.

The CPU utilization is very high at 92%, while I/O time ratio is low at 9%, indicating that the workflow is spending most of its time computing rather than waiting on I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization and network utilization are both very low, ruling out I/O or network bottlenecks. Therefore, the primary bottleneck is compute bound.

The CPU utilization is very high at 88%, while I/O time accounts for only 12% of total time, indicating compute dominates runtime. Storage bandwidth and network utilization are low, showing no I/O or network bottleneck. The workload is limited by CPU compute rather than I/O or data movement.

The CPU utilization is very high at 85%, while I/O time is only 10% of total time and storage bandwidth utilization is low at 13%. Network usage is minimal, indicating no network bottleneck. These signals show that the workflow is primarily limited by compute rather than I/O or network resources.

The CPU utilization is very high at 94%, while I/O time is only 6% of total time and storage bandwidth utilization is very low at 8%. Network usage is negligible. This indicates that the workflow is primarily limited by computation rather than I/O or network factors.

The CPU utilization is very high at 96%, while I/O time is only 4% of total time, indicating compute dominates runtime. Storage bandwidth and network utilization are low, so I/O is not limiting performance. The workload is clearly compute bound.

The CPU utilization is very high at 90%, while I/O time ratio is low at 9%, indicating that the workflow is mostly compute-bound. Storage bandwidth utilization is only 15%, and network utilization is negligible, so neither storage nor network is a bottleneck. The I/O pattern shows large sequential transfers with low IOPS, further supporting that I/O is not limiting performance.

The CPU utilization is high at 82%, while I/O time ratio is low at 18%, indicating the workflow spends most time computing rather than waiting on I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization is low (23%) and network usage is minimal, so neither storage nor network are bottlenecks. This points to compute being the primary limiting factor.

The CPU utilization is very high at 87%, while I/O time ratio is low at 11%, indicating that the workload is primarily limited by computation rather than I/O. Storage bandwidth utilization is low (22%) and network utilization is minimal (1%), so neither storage nor network are bottlenecks. This points to the compute phase dominating the runtime.
